planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil reaches 65°F and all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80°F; Capsicum baccatum germinates more reliably when bottom heat is maintained consistently throughout the seedling stage. Transplant to the warmest, most sheltered garden bed once nights stay above 55°F, spacing plants 18–24 inches apart in well-drained, fertile soil. Fruits ripen from green through yellow-orange to a vibrant golden yellow and should be harvested when fully colored for the characteristic fruity, berry-like heat. In frost-free climates the plants are perennial; pot and overwinter indoors elsewhere to carry productive plants to a second year.

planting.note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost to allow enough growing time; transplant only after nights are reliably above 60°F.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep and maintain a constant soil temperature of 85–90°F; germination is slow and irregular, so start more seeds than needed and be patient. Transplant to a sheltered, heat-retaining bed after all frost danger has passed, and protect young plants from cool nights with row cover. Fruits turn from green to red (or yellow in some strains) at full maturity; wear gloves and avoid touching the face when harvesting, as the heat exceeds one million Scoville units. The extremely long growing season makes this pepper best suited to zones 9–12 or greenhouse culture in cooler regions.

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed once soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in moist starting mix, maintaining soil at 85°F for best germination results; this Capsicum chinense type is among the slower starters. Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil only after the garden is fully warm, and consider black plastic mulch to boost soil temperatures in cooler climates. The bonnet-shaped fruits ripen from green through yellow, orange, or red depending on the strain and carry a distinctive fruity aroma alongside intense heat. Use gloves when handling ripe fruits; they are essential in Caribbean jerk seasoning and hot sauces.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed to at least 65°F.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and provide consistent heat of 80°F until seedlings emerge. Transplant to well-drained soil in the sunniest garden position, as Capsicum frutescens types thrive with maximum heat accumulation throughout the season. Distinctive erect fruits ripen from yellow-green through yellow and orange to red; harvest at the red stage for the highest capsaicin concentration. Plants are short-lived perennials in frost-free climates and can be overwintered in pots to produce a second heavy crop.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks after frost danger has passed once soil is warm.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 75–80°F and transplant after last frost into fertile, well-drained soil. This Italian heirloom from Basilicata bears long, thin-walled, crinkled fruits that dangle in clusters and turn from green to brilliant red at maturity. The fruits are nearly heat-free and intensely sweet, making them outstanding for frying in olive oil, roasting, or drying. Harvest when fully red for peak sweetness, or dry on a string in a warm, airy location — the thin walls dehydrate rapidly and concentrate flavor.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix and transplant to fertile, well-drained garden soil once the risk of frost has fully passed. The heart-shaped, thick-walled fruits ripen from green to deep red and are sweeter and more aromatic than most bell peppers, with a rich, almost spicy fragrance that is distinct to the type. Harvest at full red for fresh use, or roast and peel for preserving in oil — pimento is the classic stuffing for green olives. High yields from compact plants make this a productive choice for containers or small beds.

planting.note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost; unlike most peppers, rocoto prefers cool nights and actually performs poorly in intense summer heat.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep at a cooler 65–70°F — unlike other peppers, Capsicum pubescens actually germinates better in cooler soil and is damaged by excessive heat. This Andean species is unique among cultivated peppers for its purple flowers and distinctive black seeds; it tolerates light frost better than any other pepper species. Transplant to a position with afternoon shade in hot climates, as rocoto stops setting fruit when temperatures exceed 90°F — coastal or mountain gardens suit it best. Fruits resemble small apples (hence 'manzano') and ripen to yellow, orange, or red with thick, sweet flesh and intense, burning heat.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80°F and maintain consistent warmth until transplanting time. Plants grow tall and may benefit from staking; set into fertile, well-drained soil after last frost and side-dress with a balanced fertilizer when flowering begins. Long, narrow pods ripen from green to dark glossy red and must be fully dried for authentic guajillo chile flavor — harvest red ripe pods and dry in a food dehydrator or warm oven. The dried pods develop a distinctive tangy, berry-like flavor essential in Mexican mole and enchilada sauces.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches 65°F; count back from first fall frost to confirm enough growing days.

directions: Sow 2–3 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in deeply amended, compost-rich soil and thin to the strongest vine after the first true leaves appear. Kabocha is a Japanese winter squash of the Cucurbita maxima species; its flattened, dark-green fruits have dense, dry, sweet orange flesh superior to many other squash types. Harvest when the skin is very hard, the stem corky and dry, and a small section of rind scratched with a fingernail shows no damage. Cure at room temperature for 10–14 days before storing; properly cured kabocha keeps four to six months in a cool, dry room.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil has warmed to 65°F; Hubbard requires a long, warm season so time carefully against fall frost dates.

directions: Plant 3–4 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in deeply worked, fertile soil, thinning to two vines after establishment. Hubbard squash produces very large, warty fruits with a hard blue-gray or orange shell and very sweet, dry, fine-grained flesh ideal for pie and soup. Allow vines to spread freely over 8–12 feet of ground, or train onto a very strong trellis and support individual fruits in slings. Fruits are ripe when the rind resists a fingernail, the skin has turned its characteristic color, and the stem is fully dry; cure for two weeks before storing.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks after last frost once soil warms to 65°F; in short-season areas, start indoors 3 weeks before transplanting.

directions: Sow 2–3 seeds 1 inch deep per hill in compost-enriched soil and thin to one or two vines per hill once established. Red Kuri is a teardrop-shaped Cucurbita maxima with vivid orange-red skin and nutty, chestnut-flavored, dry flesh that is among the finest of all winter squash. Harvest when skin is deep orange-red and hard; the stems will be beginning to dry and the skin will resist a thumbnail. Cure for 10–14 days at room temperature before storing; fruits keep well for three to four months under cool, dry conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–3 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 75°F; a truly tropical vine requiring a long, hot summer.

directions: Nick the seed coat or soak seeds 24 hours before sowing 1/2 inch deep to improve germination rates in this heat-demanding tropical. Provide a tall, sturdy trellis or overhead structure — vines can exceed 12 feet and produce pendulous fruits that must hang freely to grow straight, reaching 1–3 feet in length. Harvest young fruits at 12–18 inches while skin is still soft and pale green-striped white; the tender young fruit is used like summer squash in South and Southeast Asian cooking. Overripe fruits develop an unpleasant bitter compound and turn orange-red, signaling they should be discarded rather than eaten.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 75°F; requires sustained heat to produce well.

directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in warm soil after last frost and provide a trellis or fence immediately, as vines climb vigorously by tendrils. Ridge gourd differs from smooth luffa in having ten prominent sharp ridges running the length of the fruit and slightly more tender flesh when young. Harvest fruits at 6–9 inches long while skin is still soft and the ridges are firm and green — the young flesh is mild, slightly sweet, and commonly used in Indian dal, stir-fries, and curries. Like smooth luffa, fruits left to fully mature develop a tough fiber skeleton and can serve as a scrubbing sponge.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant stem cuttings 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil and water temperatures are consistently warm.

directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep or root stem cuttings in moist to waterlogged soil; this semi-aquatic vine thrives in boggy beds, the margins of ponds, or in containers of shallow water. Harvest young shoot tips and tender leaves 4–6 inches long by snipping with scissors; plants regrow quickly and can be cut every 2–3 weeks throughout the warm season. The hollow stems and arrowhead-shaped leaves are a staple of Thai, Filipino, Chinese, and Indonesian cooking, most famously stir-fried with garlic and shrimp paste. Note that cultivation is restricted in some U.S. states due to invasive potential in warm climates; check local regulations before planting outdoors.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost once soil is consistently warm; the heat-season replacement for spinach in tropical and subtropical gardens.

directions: Scatter seeds thinly on the soil surface — they need light to germinate — then press gently and keep moist; thin seedlings to 6 inches apart, using thinnings as baby greens. Leaves are brilliantly colored in shades of red, purple, and green and are mildly flavored, used in stir-fries, soups, and as a cooked green throughout South and Southeast Asia. Harvest outer leaves or cut entire young plants at 6–8 inches tall to encourage regrowth; plants that are allowed to flower and set seed will readily self-sow for the following year. This species thrives in the same hot, humid conditions that cause true spinach to bolt immediately.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil warms to at least 65°F and all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80–85°F and maintain consistent heat throughout the seedling stage; Thai eggplant germinates and grows faster than larger-fruited types. Transplant to a warm, sheltered bed after last frost and mulch heavily to retain soil warmth. The round, golf ball-sized fruits ripen from white-striped green to fully white or yellow depending on the cultivar, and are harvested while still firm and slightly underripe for use in Thai green curry. Overripe fruits become bitter; check plants every 2–3 days and harvest promptly to keep plants productive.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant once soil reaches 70°F and nights are reliably above 55°F.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in warm starting mix at 80°F and provide consistent warmth until transplanting. This West African species produces small, rounded fruits in white, green, yellow, or red and is more heat and drought tolerant than common eggplant; it is widely grown as a staple vegetable across sub-Saharan Africa. Harvest fruits while skin is still glossy and firm — they are best eaten cooked in stews, soups, and sauces where their slightly bitter flavor mellows and complements. Plants are perennial in frost-free climates and can be cut back hard after the main season to produce a second flush of fruiting growth.

planting.note: Plant corms at or just after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; in zones 8–11 taro may stay in the ground year-round.

directions: Plant small corms or corm sections 2–3 inches deep in moist to wet, organically rich soil; taro grows naturally in waterlogged conditions and tolerates standing water at the margins of ponds and streams. Provide afternoon shade in the hottest climates to prevent leaf scorch, and keep the soil consistently moist throughout the growing season — drought stress greatly reduces corm size. Harvest when the large shield-shaped leaves begin to yellow and die back, typically 6–9 months after planting, carefully digging corms to avoid damage. The corms must be thoroughly cooked before eating to neutralize calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense mouth irritation in raw form; young leaves and stems are also edible when cooked.

planting.note: Plant rhizome sections in still or slow-moving water 1–2 feet deep after last frost when water temperature stays above 65°F.

directions: Plant intact rhizome sections (with at least one growing tip) horizontally in the mud bottom of a pond, tub, or container submerged under 12–18 inches of still water; never allow the growing tip to dry out. Full sun is essential — lotus will produce only foliage and few flowers in shade. Harvest rhizomes in fall once foliage dies back by reaching into the mud and carefully pulling out the chain of large, starchy sections; the distinctive lacy, hole-patterned cross-section is a signature feature. Rhizomes must be cooked before eating; they are eaten stir-fried, pickled, or braised throughout East and Southeast Asia and have a crunchy texture even after cooking.

planting.note: Plant sprouted corms 2–4 weeks after last frost once water temperatures exceed 60°F in a container or shallow pond.

directions: Sprout small corms in a tray of moist soil indoors a few weeks before transplanting; plant sprouted corms 1–2 inches deep in the mud bottom of containers, tubs, or shallow water features kept filled to 2–4 inches above the soil surface. Water chestnuts are tropical grass-like sedges grown for their crunchy, sweet corms that form in the mud at the base of the rush-like stems. Drain the growing container in early fall before frost and allow the foliage to die back completely, then harvest corms by sifting through the mud; the crisp, mild corms can be eaten raw or cooked and retain their crunch even after stir-frying. In frost-prone climates store harvested corms in moist sand in a cool (40°F) location and re-plant the following spring.

planting.note: Plant tuber sections or bulbils in spring after last frost once soil reaches 60°F; established crowns are hardy to zone 5.

directions: Plant tuber sections or aerial bulbils (small potato-like growths that form in the leaf axils) 2–3 inches deep in deep, loose, stone-free soil; the main tuber can grow 3 feet straight down, so deep, well-prepared beds are essential. Provide a trellis or fence immediately as this vigorous twining vine can reach 15 feet in a season. Harvest main tubers in late fall after the vine dies back, being careful to dig deeply and extract the entire tuber without breaking; the secondary bulbils can also be harvested and eaten or saved for replanting. The raw tuber is one of the few yams that can be eaten uncooked and grated — called nagaimo in Japanese cuisine — where its distinctive gluey texture is prized.

planting.note: Plant tubers just before to just after last frost — oca needs a long, cool season and tuber formation is triggered by shortening day length in autumn.

directions: Plant small tubers 3 inches deep in loose, well-drained soil in early spring; oca is an Andean crop from the clover family and grows best in cool, moist conditions similar to potato, but requires a very long growing season. The plants produce clover-like foliage and only initiate tuber formation in response to the short days of autumn, so harvesting cannot occur until after the first frosts kill the foliage — typically November. Harvest tubers by digging carefully after frost kills the tops; tubers range from white to yellow, orange, red, and purple depending on the variety. Fresh-dug tubers are acidic and starchy but sweeten after a week of sun exposure that converts starch to sugars; eat roasted, boiled, or raw once sweetened.

planting.note: Plant tubers at or just before last frost in cool, moist soil; ulluco initiates tubers only as day length shortens in fall.

directions: Plant small seed tubers 3 inches deep in loose, fertile, well-drained soil as early in spring as soil can be worked; ulluco is an Andean root crop closely related to beets that prefers cool, foggy conditions and struggles in summer heat above 80°F. The attractive heart-shaped leaves are glossy and edible as salad greens throughout the season, adding a mild, slightly mucilaginous element similar to malabar spinach. Tuber formation is triggered by short days in autumn, so harvest cannot begin until after the first frost kills the foliage, typically in October or November. The small, colorful tubers (yellow, orange, red, or purple-streaked) are waxy and hold their shape when cooked; boil, roast, or stir-fry in the same way as fingerling potatoes.

planting.note: Transplant divisions or plant fresh seed in fall under deciduous hardwood canopy; ramps are woodland ephemerals that emerge in early spring and die back by early summer.

directions: Plant bulb divisions 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich woodland soil under deciduous trees that provide deep summer shade; ramps are native North American woodland plants and require the ephemeral light window of early spring before tree leaf-out. Seed germinates slowly and erratically, often taking 18 months for full emergence after a double dormancy period requiring warm then cold stratification; vegetative division of established clumps is the most reliable propagation method. Harvest sparingly — take no more than one leaf per plant or one bulb in three, leaving the colony to regenerate — as over-harvesting wild populations is a significant ecological concern. The entire plant (broad flat leaves, small white bulb, and stems) carries a strong, complex garlic-onion flavor and is celebrated as a spring delicacy across Appalachia.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 4–6 weeks before hard freeze in zones 5–9; in zones 3–4, plant as early as possible in spring.

directions: Plant individual bulbs 1 inch deep in well-drained, fertile soil — large bulbs planted in fall will multiply into a cluster of 8–12 medium onions, while small bulbs tend to produce fewer but larger bulbs. Potato onions are a multiplier onion type that propagates entirely by bulb division, forming a cluster of bulbs in the same way shallots do; no seed is needed or typically produced. Allow tops to fall over and begin to yellow before harvesting the entire cluster; cure in a warm, airy spot for 2–3 weeks. Save the smallest bulbs from the cluster for replanting, perpetuating the variety indefinitely without seed purchases.

planting.note: Plant cloves in fall 2–4 weeks before hard ground freeze to allow root establishment before winter dormancy.

directions: Plant individual cloves 2–4 inches deep pointed end up in well-drained, fertile soil in fall, with the flat base downward. Elephant garlic is technically a leek relative (Allium ampeloprasum) rather than a true garlic; its flavor is much milder and sweeter than hardneck garlic, lacking the pungent bite, and the huge cloves are easy to peel. Remove flower scapes (curling stems) in early summer to redirect energy into bulb development, just as with true garlic. Harvest when the lower third of the leaves has browned, dig carefully, and cure in a warm, ventilated location for 3–4 weeks before storing or braiding.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 70°F; a truly tropical legume requiring a long, warm, humid season.

directions: Scarify or soak seeds 12–24 hours before sowing 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained, low-fertility soil; inoculate with cowpea rhizobium to promote nitrogen fixation. Provide a sturdy trellis or fence at least 6 feet tall as soon as plants emerge, as vines climb rapidly and are very productive when vertical. Virtually every part of the winged bean is edible — harvest young pods at 3–4 inches with frilly wings still tender, leaves and shoot tips as greens, flowers as a colorful salad garnish, and mature dried seeds as high-protein pulses. In the tropics, tuberous roots also form and can be harvested and eaten like yam.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–6 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; tepary bean is a drought-adapted desert crop that actually produces poorly in wet soils.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep directly into very well-drained, low-fertility, sandy or rocky desert-type soil after the soil is thoroughly warm; avoid overwatering, as excess moisture promotes disease and poor productivity in this drought-adapted species. Tepary bean is an ancient domesticate of the Sonoran Desert and O'odham peoples, capable of completing its life cycle on surprisingly little water. Both vining and bush types exist; most modern varieties are semi-vining and benefit from support. Harvest entire plants when the majority of pods have dried and rattled, thresh the seeds, and dry thoroughly before storage; the small seeds (white, brown, tan, or speckled) are high in protein and have a nutty, earthy flavor excellent in Southwest-style soups and stews.

planting.note: Direct sow 3–6 weeks before last frost for spring; sow again 6–8 weeks before first fall frost for the best-flavored autumn harvest.

directions: Scatter seeds thinly and cover with 1/4 inch of soil in a cool, moist seedbed; germination is rapid in cool conditions. Red mizuna has the same deeply-cut, feathery leaf form as green mizuna but with vivid burgundy-red stems and leaf coloring that intensifies in cool weather, making it as ornamental as it is edible. Harvest outer leaves continuously once plants reach 4 inches, or cut the entire plant 1 inch above the base to allow regrowth; flavor is peppery and nutty, similar to green mizuna but with slightly more bite. Fall sowings consistently outperform spring plantings in flavor and color intensity as frost sweetens the leaves.

planting.note: Sow 8–10 weeks before first fall frost for the sweetest, most vibrant roots; spring plantings are possible but fall is strongly preferred.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in loose, deeply prepared, stone-free soil in late summer or early fall; thin seedlings to 4–6 inches for proper root development. Watermelon radish is a Chinese heirloom daikon type with a pale green exterior that conceals a vivid magenta-to-pink interior resembling watermelon flesh. Fall planting is strongly preferred over spring — cool nights concentrate sugars and deepen the interior color while reducing pungency. Harvest when roots reach 2–3 inches in diameter; slice thinly for salads, pickle in rice vinegar, or roast where the color intensifies to brilliant crimson.

planting.note: Sow 8–10 weeks before first fall frost; black radish stores well into winter and is best harvested after light frosts improve flavor.

directions: Sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in loose, deeply prepared, fertile soil in late summer for fall harvest; thin seedlings to 6–8 inches to allow the large roots to develop fully. Black radish is an ancient European winter storage root with rough, jet-black skin and crisp, pungent white flesh — significantly hotter than spring radishes and reminiscent of strong horseradish. Harvest after light frost, which mellows the intense pungency; roots can reach 3–4 inches in diameter and 4–6 inches long. The robust flavor is best appreciated when the flesh is grated raw into salads with sour cream, pickled in brine, or cooked in soups where the heat moderates pleasantly.

planting.note: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in spring; the long growing season requires an early start and the slow germination period means pre-soaking seeds is beneficial.

directions: Soak seeds in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination; sow 1/4 inch deep in deeply loosened, stone-free soil in rows, as seeds germinate slowly and unevenly over 2–4 weeks. Thin seedlings to 6–8 inches apart once established; the flat-leaf parsley-like foliage is edible throughout the season as an herb. The ivory-white, parsnip-like root develops slowly all season and tastes like a concentrated, slightly sweet parsley-parsnip hybrid — exceptional in Central European soups and stews. Harvest in fall after light frost improves sweetness; roots left in the ground over winter in milder climates will re-sprout in spring but roots lose quality as the plant prepares to bolt.

planting.note: Plant root cuttings (thongs) in early spring; plants establish slowly and should not be harvested until the second or third year.

directions: Plant root cuttings (thongs) 2–3 inches deep in deep, well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil with good lime content mimicking its native coastal habitat; sea kale is extremely drought and salt tolerant once established. The harvest is the blanched young shoots produced in early spring — cover crowns with a large pot or bucket to exclude light in late winter, and cut the pale, tender shoots when 6–8 inches tall. After harvesting the blanched shoots, remove the cover to allow the plant to photosynthesize and rebuild energy for the following year — do not blanch the same crown more than two consecutive years. The blanched shoots taste like a delicate cross between asparagus and cauliflower and have been a British garden delicacy since the 18th century.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; fresh seed is critical as viability drops sharply after 6 months — sow generously.

directions: Sow very fresh seed 1/4 inch deep thickly in cool, well-drained soil, as germination rates drop dramatically when seed is more than a few months old — plan to sow thickly and use thinnings. Agretti is an Italian coastal plant related to glasswort, and it thrives in sandy, saline, or alkaline soils where most vegetables struggle; it is very intolerant of waterlogged conditions. Harvest young shoot tips and the grass-like, succulent leaves when 6–8 inches tall by snipping with scissors 1–2 inches above the soil, allowing the plant to regrow once or twice before it goes to seed. The slender, fleshy, bright-green leaves have a mildly salty, slightly mineral flavor outstanding blanched with lemon or served alongside fish and seafood in the Italian spring tradition.
